serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Kashmiri Rogan Josh is approximately 300 g, comprising braised lamb pieces in a rich spiced gravy, served as a main course alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton curry (Indian preparation) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not list Kashmiri Rogan Josh specifically. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton curry (cooked, with gravy) entry, which is the closest approved primary source for a slow-cooked Indian lamb/mutton curry. Kashmiri Rogan Josh uses similar lamb-to-gravy ratios and comparable spice and fat levels. Sodium is adjusted upward slightly to reflect restaurant preparation. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Lamb, composite of trimmed retail cuts, cooked) was used as a cross-reference for protein and fat values.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-based curry dishes — analogous estimate based on lamb curry and low-carbohydrate meat stew data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kashmiri Rogan Josh in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 25 (low) based on analogous meat-based curry and stew entries in the Sydney GI Database, which consistently show GI values in the 20–35 range for low-carbohydrate meat dishes with spiced gravy. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate (3.8 g per 100 g), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 2.9 per 300 g serving (GL = GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving / 100 = 25 × 11.4 / 100 ≈ 2.9). This is consistent with the low-GI classification for high-protein, high-fat meat dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from restaurant preparation and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Kashmiri Rogan Josh provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 25 and only 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, Kashmiri Rogan Josh has a negligible glycemic load and is unlikely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lamb and cooking fat (ghee or mustard oil)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 300 g serving contributes approximately 12.3 g saturated fat, approaching the 14 g daily upper limit for saturated fat in cardiovascular risk management; individuals with established CVD should limit portion size and prefer preparations using minimal ghee.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kashmiri Rogan Josh is a celebrated slow-cooked lamb curry originating from the Kashmir Valley of northern India, and is one of the signature dishes of Kashmiri Wazwan cuisine. The dish is prepared by braising bone-in or boneless lamb pieces in a deeply aromatic gravy built from Kashmiri red chillies (which impart a vivid crimson colour without excessive heat), dried ginger powder (sonth), fennel powder (saunf), whole spices including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaves, and often a small amount of asafoetida (hing). Yogurt is incorporated to enrich and thicken the gravy, and the dish is traditionally cooked in mustard oil or ghee. The result is tender, fall-off-the-bone lamb in a rich, fragrant, moderately spiced red gravy with a complex layering of warm spice notes, mild heat, and a slightly tangy depth from the yogurt. Nutritionally, Kashmiri Rogan Josh is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with very low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-glycaemic dietary patterns. Per 100 g it provides approximately 165 kcal, 13.5 g protein, 11.2 g fat, and 3.8 g carbohydrate. The dish is a meaningful source of iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and phosphorus from the lamb. Sodium content is elevated in restaurant preparations and warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 25 (low), and the glycaemic load per standard serving is negligible, making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when paired with low-GI accompaniments. The saturated fat content from lamb and cooking fat is a consideration for cardiovascular disease management. In Singapore, Kashmiri Rogan Josh is served at Indian restaurants and available through food delivery platforms including field survey.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Rogan Josh?
Is Rogan Josh good for diabetics?
What spices are in Rogan Josh?
What is the difference between Rogan Josh and regular lamb curry?
How much protein in Rogan Josh?
Is Rogan Josh high in sodium?

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN does not publish a specific Kashmiri Rogan Josh entry; nutrient values are adapted from the closest approved primary source (ICMR-NIN mutton curry, cooked with gravy) with cross-reference to USDA SR Legacy lamb data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using comparable low-carbohydrate meat curry entries. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish match exists in any approved primary source.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton curry (cooked, with gravy) as the closest approved primary source; no exact Kashmiri Rogan Josh entry exists in ICMR-NIN, USDA, or HPB Singapore databases. Sodium value (420 mg per 100 g) reflects restaurant-style preparation and is higher than home-cooked estimates. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this dish category. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy lamb cooked composite and ICMR-NIN spiced meat dish data; these are marked as adapted. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database meat curry entries; no direct GI measurement for Kashmiri Rogan Josh is available. The dish is typically halal as lamb is used; halal status depends on restaurant certification. Gluten-free status assumes no wheat-based thickeners are used, which is traditional but may vary by restaurant. The input record listed cuisine_tags including Biryani, Butter Chicken, and Naan which are unrelated dishes and have been excluded from this record.
